The front leg works the glutes and external rotators (piriformis); the back leg works the hip flexors and internal rotators. After heavy squats, deadlifts, or hip work, this is the single most complete hip mobility exercise available.
tip: If your back hip keeps lifting, don't force it down — sit on a folded towel or yoga block instead. The rotation must come from the hip, not the spine. Getting the position right at 70% depth beats forcing end range with compensation.

name: Piriformis / Figure-4 Stretch
steps: Lie on your back, knees bent, feet flat.
Cross your right ankle over your left knee (figure-4 position).
Reach through the gap and clasp behind your left thigh.
Gently pull your left thigh toward your chest.
Hold and breathe. Switch sides.
why: The piriformis is a small but powerful external hip rotator sitting directly over the sciatic nerve. After squats it is heavily loaded as a hip stabiliser. When shortened and inflamed it compresses the sciatic nerve, causing gluteal pain. The figure-4 is the only simple supine piriformis stretch.
tip: Flex the top foot (toes toward shin) to protect the knee and increase the external rotation component.

name: Wall Calf Stretch
steps: Place both hands on a wall, one foot back 60–90 cm.
Straight-leg: keep back leg straight, press heel to floor — targets gastrocnemius.
Bent-leg: slightly bend back knee, heel still down — targets soleus.
Lean hips gently toward the wall.
Do both variations (20 sec each) before switching sides.
why: The gastrocnemius crosses both the knee and ankle. With the knee straight it is at greatest length. Chronic gastrocnemius tightness is a primary contributor to Achilles tendinopathy and plantar fasciitis. This stretch targets the gastrocnemius - the soleus requires a bent knee.
tip: The bent-knee version targets the soleus — neglecting it is a major contributor to ankle mobility limitations.

name: Sumo Adductor Stretch
steps: Stand with feet wide (wider than shoulder-width), toes turned out 45°.
Squat down until thighs are parallel or lower.
Place elbows on inner thighs and gently press outward.
Keep chest up and back straight.
Hold and breathe — let hips sink with each exhale.
why: The five adductor muscles run from the pubic bone to various points on the femur. They are eccentrically loaded in every squat and lunge. The sumo position is the only standing stretch that places all five adductor muscles at sufficient length simultaneously.
tip: Rocking side-to-side in this position targets each adductor individually and increases overall range.

name: Frog Pose
steps: Start on all fours. Slowly slide both knees wide apart — toes should point outward in line with your knees.
Lower onto your forearms with elbows under your shoulders. Keep spine neutral.
Gently push your hips backward and downward toward the floor until you feel a deep stretch in the inner thighs.
Hold without bouncing. Breathe slowly — the adductors will release progressively over the hold.
To exit: shift weight forward onto hands first, then slowly bring knees back together. Never pull knees in while fully loaded.
why: Tight adductors are one of the most underaddressed contributors to hip instability, knee pain, and reduced squat depth. Research shows that excessive adductor tightness inhibits glute medius activation — meaning no amount of glute work fully fires if the adductors aren't mobile. This static hold addresses the deepest layer of the adductor group that shorter stretches don't reach.
tip: Exit the frog pose carefully — shift forward onto your hands first before bringing the knees together. The adductors are under maximum load in this position, and pulling them together quickly can cause a groin strain. Come out as slowly as you went in.

name: Cobra / Sphinx Stretch
targets: Rectus Abdominis · Hip Flexors · Lumbar Spine
steps: Lie face down, hands under your shoulders.
Sphinx: press forearms down and lift chest — gentle spinal curve.
Cobra: straighten arms further to increase extension (if comfortable).
Keep hips and thighs pressed into the floor.
Hold, breathe, and let your belly relax downward.
why: Any core work creates a shortening bias across the anterior abdominal wall. Cobra reverses this by extending the spine while the hip stays fixed. The anterior hip capsule and hip flexors are simultaneously stretched, addressing the chronic tightness from sit-up patterns and squat sessions.
tip: Walking hands slightly to one side laterally flexes the spine for a deeper oblique stretch.

name: Forearm Flexor & Extensor Stretch
steps: Extend one arm forward, palm facing up.
Use your other hand to gently pull fingers downward — flexor stretch. Hold 30 sec.
Flip the palm down, pull fingers upward toward you — extensor stretch. Hold 30 sec.
Switch arms.
Keep elbow fully extended throughout both positions.
why: Forearm flexors and extensors are loaded in every pulling and pressing movement. Both connect via tendons at the medial and lateral epicondyle. Chronically tight forearms without stretching is the most common cause of both medial and lateral epicondylitis in gym lifters.
tip: Neglecting the extensors is a major contributor to lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow) — always do both directions.

name: Kneeling Tibialis Stretch
steps: Kneel on a soft surface, tops of feet flat on the floor.
Sit back onto your heels — feel the stretch along the front of the shin.
For one side: extend one leg back, top of foot on floor.
Use your hand to gently push the foot further into plantarflexion.
Hold, then switch sides.
why: The tibialis anterior is the antagonist to the calf complex. After tibialis raises or squat sessions it is loaded isometrically. The kneeling position places the ankle in full plantarflexion - the exact opposite of tibialis contraction - stretching it completely.
tip: Pointing the toes while kneeling deepens the stretch into the extensor digitorum and toe extensors.

name: IT Band Cross-Leg Stretch
steps: Stand near a wall for balance.
Cross the right foot in front of the left.
Shift your right hip out to the side and lean your torso away.
Feel the pull along the outer hip and thigh.
Hold, then switch sides.
why: The IT band is fascia and cannot be stretched - but the TFL and glute medius that feed tension into it can be. After unilateral leg work the TFL is shortened. The cross-leg position creates a lateral pelvic tilt placing the TFL and IT band proximal attachments under passive tension.
tip: Extending the arm of the stretching side overhead and reaching away deepens the lateral chain stretch significantly.

name: Thoracic Extension over Chair/Foam Roller
steps: Sit in a chair, place the top of the backrest at your mid-back (T6–T8 region).
Clasp hands behind your head to support the neck — never pull on it.
Slowly extend backwards over the edge, letting gravity open the thoracic spine.
Hold 15 sec, then slide up one vertebra and repeat — work 3 segments total.
Alternatively, use a foam roller on the floor for the same effect.
why: The 12 thoracic vertebrae should each have approximately 3 degrees of extension. After pressing, rowing, or deadlift work these segments are compressed into flexion. Thoracic extension over a pivot applies a targeted force at a specific vertebral level. Fundamental to shoulder health and breathing mechanics.
tip: The thoracic spine is the most under-mobilised region for gym lifters. Every vertebral level has its own stiffness — work each one separately rather than one long passive hold.

name: Seated Spinal Twist
steps: Sit tall on the floor, both legs extended.
Bend your right knee and cross it over the left leg, foot flat on the floor.
Place your left elbow on the outside of the right knee as a lever.
Rotate your torso to the right as far as comfortable — look over your right shoulder.
Hold, then switch sides — each exhale, deepen the rotation 2–3°.
why: The rhomboids and mid-trapezius are the most undertreated muscles in back training. They retract the scapula during every rowing movement but are almost never stretched. The seated twist is the most effective single stretch for them — the elbow lever prevents the shoulder from compensating.
tip: This is the best single stretch for the rhomboids and mid-traps — muscles that work intensely during all rows and pull-downs but are virtually never stretched directly.

name: Doorway Rhomboid Stretch
steps: Stand in a doorway or hold a rack upright at hip height with both hands.
Step back until your arms are fully extended — let your weight hang forward.
Round your upper back deliberately, letting the shoulder blades spread apart.
Drop your head between your arms and tuck your chin to your chest.
Hold and breathe — feel the stretch across the entire upper-mid back.
why: The rhomboids lie between the spine and the medial border of the scapula. To stretch them, the scapulae must protract by rounding the upper back. Most post-workout stretches keep the chest open, which is the wrong direction for rhomboids.
tip: The key is rounding the upper back actively — if you stay flat, you get lat stretch, not rhomboid. Think "cat pose" through the thoracic spine while hanging.

name: Kneeling Back Extension Stretch
steps: Kneel on all fours, wrists under shoulders, knees under hips.
Sit your hips back toward your heels while keeping both arms extended forward.
Walk hands slightly left to target the right side of the lower back, hold 20 sec.
Walk hands right to target the left QL, hold 20 sec.
Return to centre and breathe into the lower back for a final 15 sec hold.
why: The quadratus lumborum connects the 12th rib to the iliac crest. It is the primary lumbar stabiliser in all heavy compound lifts. Chronic QL tightness manifests as lateral lower back pain. The lateral arm walkout specifically stretches the QL on the opposite side — something straight child pose misses.
tip: The quadratus lumborum (QL) is the most common source of lower back tightness after deadlifts and rows — the lateral walkout is essential, not optional.

name: Wall Tricep Press Stretch
steps: Stand side-on to a wall, arm extended straight at shoulder height, palm flat on wall.
Keep elbow locked straight — the stretch is at the lateral elbow and outer tricep.
Slowly rotate your body away from the wall without bending the elbow.
You should feel a distinct pull along the outer edge of the upper arm.
Hold, then switch sides.
why: The lateral head of the triceps only crosses the elbow. Stretching it requires combined shoulder extension and forearm supination under a fixed-elbow condition. The wall press achieves this by pinning the wrist while the body rotates away.
tip: This isolates the lateral head specifically — the only stretch that does. Most people have never felt this one before. Go slowly on the first rep.

name: Behind-Back Tricep Stretch
targets: Medial Head · Elbow Joint Capsule · Anconeus
steps: Reach one arm behind your back from below (as if scratching your lower back).
Use your other hand to gently push the bent elbow toward the centre of your back.
Keep your chest up — don't hunch forward to compensate.
You should feel the stretch deep inside the upper arm near the elbow.
Hold, then switch arms.
why: The medial head is the deepest of the three tricep heads. It can only be fully lengthened when the shoulder is in internal rotation and the elbow is maximally flexed simultaneously. Standard overhead stretches fail to reach this head entirely.
tip: The medial head sits deepest of the three heads and is the hardest to isolate. This is the only position that reliably reaches it — don't skip it after high-volume tricep work.

name: Cross-Chest Tricep Stretch
targets: Lateral Head · Medial Head · Posterior Shoulder
steps: Bring one arm straight across your chest, elbow slightly bent (not the shoulder stretch — lower the arm 10–15°).
Hook your opposite forearm under the elbow and pull across and slightly downward.
Externally rotate the working arm (turn palm up) to shift load toward the lateral head.
Feel the stretch along the back of the upper arm, not the posterior shoulder.
Hold, switch. If you feel it in the shoulder, lower the arm slightly.
why: The angle is the key variable. At shoulder height this becomes a posterior deltoid stretch. At 10-15 degrees below shoulder height with elbow bent, the line of pull shifts to the lateral and medial tricep heads. External rotation of the forearm further engages the lateral head.
tip: The angle distinction matters: arm at shoulder height = posterior deltoid stretch. Arm 10–15° lower with elbow bent = lateral and medial tricep heads. Small adjustment, completely different target.

name: Upper Pec / High-Arm Doorway Stretch
steps: Stand in a doorframe. Place your arm high — elbow above shoulder height on the frame.
Step through and rotate your chest away until you feel the stretch high near the collarbone.
This is distinct from the mid-pec version — the elevated arm position is essential.
Alternative: lie face down, place palm beside chest and gently push the torso up, hips down.
Hold, breathe, and switch sides.
why: The pectoralis major clavicular head runs at roughly 30 degrees upward from the sternum to the humerus. To stretch it, the arm must be elevated above shoulder level. Standard doorway stretches at shoulder height miss this head almost entirely, leaving the upper pec chronically shortened after incline pressing.
tip: Most people only stretch the mid pec. The clavicular head runs from the collarbone — you must raise the arm above shoulder level to reach it. Three different arm heights = three different pec regions.

name: Pec Minor / Low-Arm Chest Stretch
targets: Pectoralis Minor · Lower Pec (Costal Head) · Serratus Anterior
steps: Stand beside a wall. Place your arm low — hand at hip height, elbow pointing behind you.
Lean your body forward and away from the wall, rotating the chest open.
Feel the stretch low across the pec toward the lower ribs — not the front of the shoulder.
Add a slight forward body lean to increase tension on the pec minor.
Hold, then switch sides.
why: The pec minor is a separate muscle from the pec major, running from the 3rd-5th ribs to the coracoid process. Its action is to pull the scapula forward and downward. After pressing it is shortened and compresses the brachial plexus. It is a primary cause of rounded shoulders in gym lifters.
tip: The pec minor sits underneath the pec major — it pulls the shoulder blade forward and down. Bench press shortens it chronically and it's a primary cause of rounded shoulders. Most stretching routines miss it entirely.

name: Prayer & Reverse Prayer Stretch
targets: Pronator Teres · Forearm Flexors · Wrist Flexors · Supinator
steps: Prayer: press palms together in front of your chest, fingers pointing up.
Slowly lower your joined hands toward your waist while keeping palms pressed — feel the wrist flexors and pronators.
Reverse prayer: flip hands so fingers point down, backs of hands together.
Gently lift the reversed hands toward your chest — stretches the extensors and supinator.
Hold each position 30 seconds.
why: Prayer position stretches the wrist flexors and pronators simultaneously. Reverse prayer is one of the only positions that reaches the supinator muscle, chronically shortened by hammer curls and neutral-grip pulling.
tip: Reverse prayer is one of the only positions that reaches the supinator muscle directly — critical after heavy curls, chin-ups, or any supination-heavy movement.

name: Passive Supination Stretch
steps: Extend one arm in front at shoulder height, elbow locked, palm facing down (pronated).
Use your other hand to grasp the back of the extended wrist.
Gently rotate the forearm palm-up (supination), assisting with the gripping hand.
Hold at end range — feel the stretch in the outer forearm below the elbow.
Hold, then switch arms.
why: The pronator teres is chronically shortened in anyone who grips heavily. When shortened it not only limits forearm supination but also compresses the median nerve in the proximal forearm - a common cause of forearm fatigue and numbness during high-rep pulling work.
tip: The pronator teres is chronically shortened in anyone who grips heavily. Releasing it improves both bicep peak contraction and wrist comfort significantly.

name: Chin Tuck (Suboccipital Release)
targets: Cervical Extensors · Suboccipital Muscles · Upper Traps
steps: Sit or stand tall with shoulders back and down.
Draw your chin straight back — not downward, directly backward — making a "double chin".
Place two fingers on your chin and gently apply backward pressure.
You should feel a stretch at the base of your skull and upper neck.
Hold 30 seconds, release fully, repeat 3 times.
why: The suboccipital muscles at the base of the skull are the most chronically tight group in the human body. They work isometrically in virtually every exercise to maintain head position against load. After any heavy traps or overhead work these muscles are in acute protective tension.
tip: The suboccipital muscles at the base of the skull are the most chronically tight group for anyone doing overhead pressing or heavy traps — and almost never stretched. This is their direct fix.

name: Neck Flexor Stretch
steps: Sit tall. Place one hand gently on your chest/collarbone to anchor the skin.
Slowly tilt your head back, leading with your chin toward the ceiling.
Keep your mouth closed — don't let your jaw hang open.
Feel a gentle stretch along the front of the throat and anterior neck.
Hold, return slowly, and repeat.
why: The anterior neck flexors are shortened during any exercise where the chin is tucked or the head pushed forward. Un-stretched, they contribute to forward-head posture that progressively loads the cervical spine with extra effective head weight per centimetre of forward displacement.
tip: The anterior neck flexors shorten during any exercise where you brace or tuck your chin. This counters the forward-head posture exaggerated by heavy lifting.

name: SCM Stretch
steps: Sit tall. Place one hand on your collarbone on the side to be stretched.
Tilt your head back and to the opposite side simultaneously.
Rotate your chin slightly upward and away from the stretching side.
Feel a distinct pull along the diagonal rope-like muscle on the side of your neck.
Hold gently — this muscle responds to light sustained pressure, not force. Switch sides.
why: The SCM runs from the sternum and clavicle to the mastoid process. After heavy trap and overhead pressing work it goes into protective tension. Chronic SCM tightness causes restricted cervical rotation, jaw tension, and headaches.
tip: SCM tightness causes headaches, jaw tension, and restricted rotation — common after heavy trap or shrug sessions. Light pressure held for 60 seconds outperforms aggressive stretching here.

name: Seated Hamstring Stretch
steps: Sit on the floor, one leg extended, the other foot tucked to inner thigh (hurdle position).
Hinge forward at the hips — keep your back flat, not rounded.
Reach toward your extended foot, holding wherever you can reach without rounding.
For a medial bias: slightly internally rotate the extended leg (toes pointing slightly inward).
Hold and breathe — deepen with each exhale. Switch sides.
why: The medial hamstrings and the lateral hamstring have different attachment points at the knee and require different leg rotation angles. Internal leg rotation biases the medial group — the most common source of posterior knee tightness after leg curls and RDLs.
tip: Internal rotation targets the medial hamstrings (semimembranosus and semitendinosus). External rotation shifts to the biceps femoris. Your standing hamstring stretch only hits one angle — you need both.

name: Seated Runner's Stretch
steps: Sit on the floor with one leg extended straight in front. Bend the other leg and tuck the foot against the inner thigh of the extended leg.
Sit tall — don't round your back before you even start. Feel the sit bones grounded.
Hinge forward from the hips, keeping your spine long, reaching toward the foot of the extended leg.
Hold at your comfortable end range — don't bounce or force the reach.
To shift emphasis: move the foot slightly inward for outer hamstring, slightly outward for inner hamstring and adductor.
why: Unlike the standard double-leg seated stretch, this unilateral version isolates each hamstring individually and — because the bent leg opens the hip — also stretches the adductor of the extended leg where it interfaces with the inner hamstring. This is the exact stretch zone that is chronically undertreated in athletes and contributes to groin and posterior knee issues.
tip: Don't chase your foot with a rounded spine — the benefit is from hip hinging, not spinal flexion. A neutral spine at 80% reach produces more hamstring length change than a rounded spine at 100%. Use a towel around the foot if needed to maintain length.

name: Supine Lying Hamstring Stretch
targets: Biceps Femoris (Long & Short Head) · Sciatic Nerve Floss
steps: Lie on your back, one leg flat on the floor.
Raise the other leg, holding behind the thigh or calf (or use a towel around the foot).
Keep the raised leg as straight as comfortable — flex the foot (toes back) for more intensity.
For biceps femoris: externally rotate the leg slightly (toes point outward).
Hold, lower slowly, switch sides.
why: The lying position decompresses the lumbar spine while stretching, making it safer after heavy deadlift sessions. The external leg rotation targets the biceps femoris — the most commonly injured hamstring muscle in gym training.
tip: The lying position decompresses the lumbar spine while stretching — safer and often more effective than standing versions after heavy deadlift or squat sessions. The external rotation targets the lateral hamstring (biceps femoris) specifically.

name: Knee to Chest Stretch
steps: Lie flat on your back on the floor. Extend one leg fully flat — keep it straight throughout.
Bend the other knee and place both hands just below the kneecap.
Gently pull the knee toward your chest until you feel a stretch in the glute and lower back.
Keep the lower back pressed to the floor — don't let it arch up as you pull.
Hold, then slowly release and switch legs. For a deeper stretch, pull both knees in together.
why: After lower body training — especially squats and hip thrusts — the glutes are shortened and the lumbar spine is compressed. The knee-to-chest stretch decompresses the lumbar vertebrae while simultaneously placing the glute at full length. Extending the opposite leg flat increases the pelvic tilt and deepens the stretch significantly.
tip: Extend the non-working leg completely flat on the floor rather than leaving it bent. This flattens the pelvis and increases the ROM in the pulled hip by 20-30% — most people miss this and wonder why the stretch feels weak.

name: Pigeon Pose
targets: Glute Medius · Glute Minimus · Piriformis · Hip External Rotators
steps: From all fours, bring your right knee forward behind your right wrist.
Slide your right foot toward your left hand — more horizontal shin = deeper stretch.
Extend the left leg straight back, top of foot on the floor.
Lower your hips toward the floor evenly — use a folded towel under the right hip if needed.
Hold upright or fold forward over the front leg. Switch sides.
why: The glute medius and minimus are critical pelvis stabilisers. Pigeon achieves the specific combination of hip flexion, external rotation, and abduction that fully lengthens the glute medius at its ilium origin. No standing stretch approximates this depth.
tip: Pigeon pose is the gold standard for glute med and min. The IT band cross-leg stretch is a quick standing approximation — pigeon is the full version. After heavy squats or leg press, this should be non-negotiable.

name: Side-Lying Abductor Stretch
targets: TFL · Glute Medius · IT Band (Proximal Origin)
steps: Lie on your right side, legs stacked, right knee bent for stability.
Raise your left leg and bring it forward, letting it drop toward the floor in front of you.
The left hip rolls forward — feel the stretch along the outer left hip and TFL.
For more intensity, use your hand to press the knee further toward the floor.
Hold, then switch sides.
why: The TFL originates at the anterior superior iliac spine and inserts into the IT band. The side-lying position allows the hip to roll into adduction and slight internal rotation — the exact opposite of TFL action — stretching it at its ASIS origin.
tip: This isolates the TFL at its origin near the hip — something the standing IT band cross doesn't fully reach. Essential after abductor machine or wide-stance squat sessions.

name: Downward Dog Calf Stretch
steps: Start in a push-up plank position.
Push hips up and back to form an inverted V shape.
Press both heels toward the floor — they don't need to touch, just reach.
Pedal the heels alternately for the first 20 seconds to loosen both sides.
Then hold both heels down simultaneously for the final 25 seconds.
why: Downward dog provides bilateral gastrocnemius stretch with knees fully extended. Body weight provides consistent progressive tension. The inverted position also decompresses the ankle joint from below, allowing the Achilles to lengthen without compressive standing load.
tip: Downward dog stretches the gastrocnemius with the knee straight. The wall calf stretch is often done with too much knee bend, accidentally targeting the soleus instead — this is the dedicated gastroc version.

name: Seated Bent-Knee Soleus Stretch
steps: Sit on the floor with one leg extended and a towel or band looped around the ball of your foot.
Bend the knee of the stretching leg to 20–30° — this removes the gastrocnemius from the equation.
Pull the towel to dorsiflex your foot (toes toward shin) with the knee bent.
Feel the stretch deep and low in the calf, closer to the ankle than the wall version.
Hold, then switch sides.
why: The soleus originates below the knee on the posterior tibia and fibula. Because it does not cross the knee, it is completely unaffected by knee position in most stretches. With the knee bent, the gastrocnemius goes slack and the soleus becomes the limiting structure.
tip: The soleus only fully stretches when the knee is bent — it attaches below the knee unlike the gastrocnemius. Straight-leg stretches miss it almost entirely. This is the fix for persistent ankle stiffness.

name: Seated Toe Extension Stretch
targets: Extensor Digitorum Longus · Extensor Hallucis · Anterior Shin
steps: Sit in a chair. Cross one foot over the opposite knee.
Hold the top of your foot and gently curl all toes downward (plantarflex them).
Then use your other hand to push the entire foot into plantarflexion (pointing the foot).
Feel the stretch along the top of the foot and running up the shin.
Hold, then switch feet.
why: The extensor digitorum longus and extensor hallucis longus are heavily loaded in tibialis raises and work requiring sustained dorsiflexion. This stretch targets the toe extensors specifically — they have a different line of pull from the tibialis anterior.
tip: The extensor digitorum and hallucis longus lift your toes during dorsiflexion. They're heavily loaded in tibialis raises and running — this is their direct release, distinct from the kneeling shin stretch which targets the tibialis anterior.

name: Wall Ankle Dorsiflexion Stretch
targets: Tibialis Anterior · Ankle Joint Capsule · Peroneals
steps: Stand facing a wall, about 10 cm away. Place one foot forward, toes touching the wall.
Keeping the heel flat on the floor, drive your knee toward the wall.
Move your foot back from the wall to increase the stretch on the anterior ankle.
This tests and develops ankle dorsiflexion range — the most important single ankle motion.
Hold, then switch sides.
why: If the knee cannot reach the wall with the foot 10 cm back, the ankle joint capsule is restricted — not just the calf muscles. Joint capsule restriction does not respond to calf stretches; it requires direct joint mobilisation under dorsiflexion load.
tip: If your knee can't reach the wall with your foot 10 cm back, your ankle mobility is meaningfully restricted — a direct contributor to knee caving during squats. Work this daily until you can pass it comfortably.
